subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF Art IV, World Bank, V-Dem, Freedom House, RSF, TI, Mo Ibrahim) + international press.
One of Africa's institutional standouts - the historic Oct 2024 election ended ~58 years of BDP rule in a peaceful first-ever turnover - now hit by a severe, structural diamond-market shock.
Standout features: top-tier governance and a democratic high-water mark, against extreme single-commodity (diamond) dependence, a ~21% adult HIV legacy, very high inequality, and chronic drought.

economic.notes: Sound institutions and low inflation, against a severe diamond-revenue collapse and near-zero diversification.

fiscal_state.notes: A fiscal deficit ~8-11% of GDP in 2025 (among SSA's widest) with reserves down ~27% on the diamond-revenue collapse - debt rising but still moderate (~40-43%). (IMF Art IV 2025; Reuters)

monetary_independence.notes: The pula's crawling peg to an SDR+rand basket gives partial managed autonomy; the BoB sets its own rate but anchors to the basket. (Bank of Botswana)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The pula is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Low and within the 3-6% target band (~2-3.7% through 2025). (Bank of Botswana)

banking_stability.notes: Sound and well-supervised; the strain is fiscal/external rather than banking, with reserve-driven liquidity tightening. (IMF)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Severe concentration - diamonds (Debswana/De Beers) dominate exports/revenue, and the 2024-25 slump exposed near-zero diversification despite a De Beers deal to 2054. (Reuters; IMF)

institutional.notes: Among Africa's strongest rule of law and public administration, with a free-ish press.

rule_of_law.notes: Among Africa's strongest - an independent judiciary upheld a clean contested election and an orderly handover. (Freedom House 2025)

civil_service_capacity.notes: A long reputation for competent, professional administration. (Mo Ibrahim; World Bank)

political_social.notes: A landmark peaceful turnover strengthening stability, against high inequality and an HIV legacy.

political_stability.notes: The peaceful 2024 alternation (80%+ turnout, immediate concession) demonstrably strengthened stability and legitimacy. (Al Jazeera; IPU)

civil_liberties.notes: Broadly protected - rated 'Free' with an improving post-election trajectory. (Freedom House 2025)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Mixed - stable for most, with friction over migrants and marginalisation of the San/Basarwa. (HRW)

social_cohesion.notes: Generally cohesive but strained by among the world's highest inequality and the HIV legacy. (World Bank)

demographics.notes: A favourable working-age share, with ~21% adult HIV prevalence a deep structural drag. (UNAIDS)

geopolitical.notes: A peaceful, non-aligned, sanction-free state deeply integrated with South Africa.

alliance_reliability.notes: A stable, predictable non-aligned partner, deeply integrated via SACU. (observed)

conflict_involvement.notes: No armed conflict - one of Africa's most peaceful states. (observed)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: No sanctions exposure, broadly open account under a managed peg. (IMF)

physical_practical.notes: Decent regional infrastructure and HIV-strong healthcare, against arid/drought exposure.

climate.notes: Arid/semi-desert; a 2023-24 El Nino drought state of emergency (SSA's worst in ~40 years), with chronic water stress. (UN OCHA)

healthcare.notes: Strong on HIV/ART (life expectancy rebound), burdened by the HIV/NCD double load and rural gaps. (WHO)

infrastructure.notes: Decent core infrastructure for the region but landlocked and water-constrained. (World Bank)

disaster_insurance.notes: High drought exposure with limited penetration and now-squeezed fiscal headroom. (inference)

summary: One of Africa's institutional standouts - the October 2024 election produced the country's first-ever peaceful party turnover after ~58 years of BDP rule, reinforcing rule of law and civil liberties. But the economy is in a genuine crisis: the global diamond slump pushed real GDP into contraction in 2025, opened one of sub-Saharan Africa's widest fiscal deficits (~8-11% of GDP), and cut reserves by roughly a quarter. Deep structural vulnerabilities - extreme single-commodity dependence, ~21% adult HIV prevalence, very high inequality, and chronic drought - make the near-term skew negative even though the long-run governance base is strong.
